content.problemStatement:
‚ÄúDo you know what someone with no money has in common with someone with too much money? Living is no fun for them.‚ÄĚ

Gi-Hun wants to know the truth about the game and II-nam has asked him to solve this puzzle in order to get answers to his questions.

Gi-Hun is given a tree consisting of NNN nodes numbered from 111 to NNN, **rooted at node 1**. Each node iii has been assigned a value AiA_iAi‚Äč.

Also given is a set of special edges SSS, consisting of some edges of the given tree (each edge is denoted by a pair of integers).
Gi-Hun is required to answer QQQ queries.

There are 3 different types of queries.


1 u K1 \ u \ K1 u K ‚ÄĒ consider the subtree of uuu in the tree, denoted by TuT_uTu‚Äč. You would like divide TuT_uTu‚Äč into KKK disconnected components by removing exactly K‚ąí1K-1K‚ąí1 **non-special** (i.e, not in SSS) edges from TuT_uTu‚Äč such that the following value is maximized:
For v‚ąąTuv \in T_uv‚ąąTu‚Äč, let ZvZ_vZv‚Äč be the number of edges removed on the path from uuu to vvv, plus 111.
Then, you would like to maximize the value
‚ąĎv‚ąąTuZv‚čÖAv \sum_{v \in T_u} Z_v\cdot A_v‚ąĎv‚ąąTu‚Äč‚ÄčZv‚Äč‚čÖAv‚Äč
If it is not possible to remove K‚ąí1K-1K‚ąí1 non-special edges from TuT_uTu‚Äč, print `IMPOSSIBLE` instead.


2 u v2 \ u \ v2 u v ‚ÄĒ add edge u‚ąívu-vu‚ąív to set SSS. It is guaranteed that this edge is part of the tree and is not already in SSS.
3 u v3 \ u \ v3 u v ‚ÄĒ remove edge u‚ąívu-vu‚ąív from set SSS. It is guaranteed that this edge is already in SSS.
**Note:** The original tree is restored after every query of type 111, i.e, edges are not permanently removed from the tree.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ, denoting the number of nodes of the tree and the number of queries respectively.
The second line contains N‚ąí1N-1N‚ąí1 space-separated integers P2,P3,‚Ä¶,PNP_2, P_3, \ldots, P_NP2‚Äč,P3‚Äč,‚Ä¶,PN‚Äč, where PiP_iPi‚Äč is the parent of node iii.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč ‚ÄĒ the values of the nodes.
The fourth line contains a single integer MMM, denoting the number of edges in the set SSS.
The next MMM lines describe the set SSS. The iii-th of these MMM lines contains two integers uiu_iui‚Äč and viv_ivi‚Äč, denoting that edge ui‚ąíviu_i-v_iui‚Äč‚ąívi‚Äč is present in SSS.
The next QQQ lines each contain 333 integers describing the queries as given in the statement.


Output Format
For each query of type 111, output on a new line the maximum value of ‚ąĎv‚ąąTuZv‚čÖAv\sum_{v\in T_u} Z_v\cdot A_v‚ąĎv‚ąąTu‚Äč‚ÄčZv‚Äč‚čÖAv‚Äč, or `IMPOSSIBLE` if there doesn't exist a way to remove K‚ąí1K-1K‚ąí1 non-special edges.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases ‚Č§ 10^5
The sum of Q over all test cases ‚Č§ 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains two integers N and Q. The second line contains N‚ÄĎ1 integers P2 ‚Ä¶ PN, where Pi is the parent of node i (the tree is rooted at 1). The third line contains N integers A1 ‚Ä¶ AN. The fourth line contains an integer M, the number of initially special edges. Each of the next M lines contains two integers u v describing a special edge (it is guaranteed to be an edge of the tree). The next Q lines describe the queries. A query is of one of three forms: 1 u K ‚Äď type‚ÄĎ1 query, 2 u v ‚Äď add edge (u,v) to the special set, 3 u v ‚Äď remove edge (u,v) from the special set. All edges given in type‚ÄĎ2/3 are tree edges.
content.outputFormat: For each query of type‚ÄĮ1 output on a separate line either the maximum possible value of ő£_{v‚ąąT_u} Z_v¬∑A_v or the word IMPOSSIBLE if it is not possible to remove K‚ÄĎ1 non‚ÄĎspecial edges inside the subtree of u.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Query 1: S={(1,3)}S = \{(1, 3)\}S={(1,3)}. Remove edges (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2) and (2,5)(2, 5)(2,5). The ZZZ-values of the nodes are [1,2,1,2,3][1, 2, 1, 2, 3][1,2,1,2,3]. This gives us ‚ąĎv‚ąąT1Zv‚čÖAv=1‚čÖ1+2‚čÖ(‚ąí3)+1‚čÖ4+2‚čÖ(‚ąí6)+3‚čÖ10=17\sum_{v\in T_1} Z_v\cdot A_v = 1\cdot 1 + 2\cdot (-3) + 1\cdot 4 + 2\cdot (-6) + 3\cdot 10 = 17‚ąĎv‚ąąT1‚Äč‚ÄčZv‚Äč‚čÖAv‚Äč=1‚čÖ1+2‚čÖ(‚ąí3)+1‚čÖ4+2‚čÖ(‚ąí6)+3‚čÖ10=17.
Query 2: Remove (1,3)(1, 3)(1,3) from SSS. SSS is now empty.
Query 3: SSS is empty. Remove edges (1,3)(1, 3)(1,3) and (2,5)(2, 5)(2,5). The ZZZ-values of the nodes are [1,1,2,1,2][1, 1, 2, 1, 2][1,1,2,1,2], which makes ‚ąĎv‚ąąT1Zv‚čÖAv=20\sum_{v\in T_1} Z_v\cdot A_v = 20‚ąĎv‚ąąT1‚Äč‚ÄčZv‚Äč‚čÖAv‚Äč=20.
Query 4: - SSS is empty. Remove the edge (2,5)(2, 5)(2,5). This gives us Z2=Z4=1Z_2 = Z_4 = 1Z2‚Äč=Z4‚Äč=1 and Z5=2Z_5 = 2Z5‚Äč=2, with the value being 1‚čÖ(‚ąí3)+1‚čÖ(‚ąí6)+2‚čÖ10=111\cdot (-3) + 1\cdot (-6) + 2\cdot 10 = 111‚čÖ(‚ąí3)+1‚čÖ(‚ąí6)+2‚čÖ10=11.
Query 5: Add (2,5)(2, 5)(2,5) to SSS. Now, S={(2,5)}S = \{(2, 5)\}S={(2,5)}.
Query 6: It is impossible to remove two non-special edges from the subtree of 222.

solution.approach:
For every node x (x‚Č†1) the edge (parent[x],x) has a fixed contribution weight w[x] = sum of A in the subtree of x. Removing this edge adds w[x] to the answer because all nodes in that subtree get their Z increased by one. Therefore a type‚ÄĎ1 query for node u and K asks for the (K‚ÄĎ1) largest weights among all edges whose child lies inside the subtree of u and that are currently *non‚ÄĎspecial*. If fewer than K‚ÄĎ1 such edges exist the answer is IMPOSSIBLE; otherwise the answer is subtreeSum[u] + sum of those (K‚ÄĎ1) largest weights.

We pre‚ÄĎcompute:
1. subtreeSum[x] for every node by a DFS.
2. An Euler tour order tin[x], tout[x] so that the subtree of x corresponds to the contiguous interval [tin[x], tout[x]] in the tour.
3. For each node x we store the weight w[x] at position tin[x] (root has no edge, store an empty list).

We build a segment tree over the Euler order. Each segment‚ÄĎtree node keeps a vector of at most 30 largest weights of its segment, sorted descending. Merging two children is done by a standard merge of two sorted vectors and truncating to the first 30 elements ‚Äď O(30).

Updates (type‚ÄĎ2 / type‚ÄĎ3) toggle the availability of a single edge. The edge is identified by its child node c; we update position tin[c] in the segment tree to either {w[c]} (available) or {} (unavailable). This costs O(log N * 30).

Query (type‚ÄĎ1) obtains the merged top‚ÄĎK list for interval [tin[u], tout[u]] by traversing O(log N) segment‚ÄĎtree nodes and merging their stored vectors (again O(30) per merge). After the traversal we have the list of the largest weights in the whole subtree. If K‚ÄĎ1 = 0 we output subtreeSum[u]; otherwise we check whether the list size ‚Č• K‚ÄĎ1. If not, IMPOSSIBLE; else answer = subtreeSum[u] + sum of the first K‚ÄĎ1 elements.

Because K ‚Č§ 30, all merges are bounded by a small constant, giving an overall complexity of O((N+Q)¬∑log N¬∑K) which easily fits the limits.

Correctness proof sketch:
*Lemma 1*: Removing an edge e = (p, c) increases Z_v by exactly 1 for every node v in the subtree of c and does not affect any other node. *Proof*: The unique path from u to any descendant of c passes through e, so the count of removed edges on that path grows by one; nodes outside the subtree of c do not use e. ‚ąé
*Lemma 2*: The total increase in the objective caused by removing a set of edges E is ő£_{e‚ąąE} w[child(e)], where w[x] = subtreeSum[x]. *Proof*: By Lemma‚ÄĮ1 each edge contributes its subtree sum once, and contributions are additive because each removal adds 1 to Z for its whole subtree independently of other removals. ‚ąé
*Lemma 3*: For a fixed node u and a fixed number of cuts t = K‚ÄĎ1, the optimal set of edges is the t edges with largest weights among all non‚ÄĎspecial edges inside T_u. *Proof*: By Lemma‚ÄĮ2 the objective is baseSum[u] + ő£_{e‚ąąE} w[e]. Maximising ő£ w[e] under the cardinality constraint |E| = t is achieved by picking the t largest available weights. ‚ąé
*Theorem*: The algorithm returns the correct answer for every type‚ÄĎ1 query, or correctly reports IMPOSSIBLE. *Proof*: The segment‚ÄĎtree query returns exactly the multiset of weights of all currently non‚ÄĎspecial edges whose child lies in T_u, sorted descending, because each leaf stores the weight of its incident edge if it is available and the internal nodes store the top‚ÄĎK of the union of their children. By Lemma‚ÄĮ3 the optimal total increase is the sum of the first K‚ÄĎ1 elements of this list, provided the list length is at least K‚ÄĎ1. Adding the invariant base sum subtreeSum[u] yields the optimal total value. If the list is shorter than K‚ÄĎ1, no feasible set of K‚ÄĎ1 edges exists, so IMPOSSIBLE is correct. ‚ąé

Thus the algorithm is both correct and efficient.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N + Q) ¬∑ log N ¬∑ K) where K ‚Č§ 30. The factor K is a small constant, so effectively O((N+Q)¬∑log N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N ¬∑ K) for the segment tree (each node stores at most 30 numbers). Additionally O(N) for the tree, Euler tour arrays and subtree sums.
